message: In sendOnce the stdin broken-pipe branch is checked before the non-zero-exit branch, so when a subprocess exits non-zero before draining all of stdin the EPIPE write error wins: Send returns a retryable transport-drop error and discards the captured stderr, instead of the terminal client error carrying the stderr diagnostic the contract requires.
trigger: Reconstructed stdin exceeds the OS pipe buffer (~64KiB) AND the subprocess exits non-zero before reading all of stdin, producing both an EPIPE on the stdin write and a non-zero wait() result in the same Send.
fix_direction: When waitErr != nil, return the terminal ErrClientError with formatStderr(stderrBuf) before the broken-pipe branch; only treat a stdin broken pipe as the retryable cause when waitErr == nil. This keeps TestSend_BrokenPipe_Retried (exit 0) green while restoring terminal classification and the stderr diagnostic for non-zero exits.
uncertainty: Whether this triggers in practice depends on whether the real agy CLI drains all of stdin before any non-zero exit; the default suite never runs real agy (only the gated integration test would), so this cannot be fully verified from the available context.

message: formatStderr labels stderr as truncated whenever len(data) >= maxStderrBytes, but data is read through io.LimitReader(stderr, maxStderrBytes) and is therefore exactly maxStderrBytes whenever stderr has at least that many bytes — including the case where the real stderr is exactly 4096 bytes and nothing was dropped, producing a misleading '(truncated)' suffix.
trigger: A subprocess exits non-zero and writes exactly maxStderrBytes (4096) bytes to stderr.
fix_direction: Detect truncation independently of the captured length, e.g. read via io.LimitReader(stderr, maxStderrBytes+1) and trim, or track whether the post-limit io.Copy drain consumed any bytes, and use that signal instead of `len(data) >= maxStderrBytes`.

message: In sendOnce the stdin broken-pipe check runs before the non-zero-exit check, so when a subprocess exits non-zero AND closes stdin before our write completes, the error is classified as a retryable transport drop (ErrTransportDrop) instead of a terminal client error (ErrClientError), and the captured stderr is dropped from the surfaced error. The contract's error-classification criterion requires a non-zero exit to be terminal with the stderr content included. This path is realistic for this backend specifically: because each Send reconstructs the full both-sided conversation context, stdin grows monotonically each round and can exceed the OS pipe buffer (~16KB on darwin, the documented target). If a real agy run rejects a request (bad model / refusal) and exits non-zero without draining oversized stdin, our write gets EPIPE; the code then returns a retryable ErrTransportDrop, retries once (same outcome), and surfaces a retryable error with no stderr — a deterministic terminal failure presented as transient and undiagnosable.
trigger: A Send whose reconstructed stdin exceeds the OS pipe buffer while the agy subprocess exits non-zero without fully draining stdin, producing a broken-pipe on our write concurrently with the non-zero exit.
fix_direction: Check the non-zero exit (waitErr) before the stdin broken-pipe, so a process that actually exited non-zero is classified terminal with stderr included; treat broken-pipe as retryable only when the process did not exit non-zero (waitErr == nil). Add a unit test combining closeStdinEarly with a non-zero exitErr asserting terminal classification and stderr presence.
uncertainty: agy's real stdin-draining behavior is only exercised by the gated integration test, so I cannot confirm agy closes stdin before exiting on rejection; if agy always reads all stdin before exiting, the broken pipe never occurs for typical sizes and the misclassification does not trigger. The contract does not explicitly specify precedence when both conditions occur simultaneously.

message: The acceptance criterion states filesystem read-only 'is not enforced by the exec backend (a plain CLI has no sandbox) and is documented as trusted/best-effort.' No such documentation exists in the package: the package doc comment, resolveCwd, and the ReadOnly handling describe only grounded-vs-sealed working-directory selection and never state that read-only is unenforced / trusted / best-effort. A maintainer reading the code could assume sealed mode provides a read-only/network sandbox when it only changes the working directory.
fix_direction: Add a doc comment (package-level or on resolveCwd / where spec.ReadOnly is consumed) stating that filesystem read-only is not enforced by this backend — a plain CLI has no sandbox — that sealed mode only relocates the working directory, and that network remains available in both modes.

message: formatStderr appends '...(truncated)' whenever len(data) >= maxStderrBytes, but data is read through io.LimitReader(stderr, maxStderrBytes) so it is capped at exactly maxStderrBytes. When the subprocess emits exactly 4096 bytes of stderr with nothing beyond it, the message is falsely labeled truncated even though no content was dropped. The drained remainder (io.Copy to io.Discard) is not consulted to decide truncation.
trigger: A non-zero exit whose captured stderr is exactly maxStderrBytes (4096) bytes with no further output.
fix_direction: Detect real truncation by reading one extra byte (e.g. io.LimitReader(stderr, maxStderrBytes+1) and trim) or by checking whether the discard drain copied >0 bytes, and only append '...(truncated)' when content was actually dropped.

message: Stale, contradictory comment in the unimplemented-backend e2e test. This change moved the fail-fast fixture to `backend: api` and registered `agy` as a resolvable backend, but the comment inside TestE2E_UnimplementedBackend_Exit1 still asserts the opposite ('defaultResolver returns error for unknown backend "agy"'). agy now resolves successfully via defaultResolver (cmd/debate/main.go:56-57), so the comment is factually wrong and is a maintenance trap: a maintainer trusting it could restore `agy` as the fixture backend, which would no longer fail fast and would silently break the test's intent.
fix_direction: Update the comment in TestE2E_UnimplementedBackend_Exit1 to reference the actual unimplemented backend ("api") instead of "agy", matching makeUnimplementedWorkspace.

message: execSession.mu mutex is unnecessary and provides only illusory thread-safety. It is locked solely in Close to guard the `closed` idempotency flag, while Send mutates s.history and reads s.spec/s.cwd unlocked. The contract assumptions explicitly state the Session is serialized by orchestrate and not required to be goroutine-safe, so a plain bool check suffices for idempotent Close and the mutex protects none of the actually-mutated state.
fix_direction: Remove sync.Mutex and guard Close idempotency with the plain `closed` bool, consistent with the contract's single-threaded-session assumption. If concurrent Close from another goroutine is genuinely intended, document it and also protect the state Send mutates (history) — and the sealed-dir removal that races the subprocess cwd.
uncertainty: The mutex may be intentional defensive double-Close safety; if so it is harmless but still does not make the session goroutine-safe, so it remains an unnecessary mechanism rather than a correctness fix.

message: New's backendID parameter is validated then discarded, creating the appearance of multi-backend support that does not exist. It is checked against BackendAgy and rejected otherwise, but never stored on execTransport, and resolveCmd hardcodes the 'agy' executable. With exactly one supported backend the parameter carries no behavior.
fix_direction: Either drop the backendID parameter since the constructor is agy-only, or, if symmetry with acp.New (which legitimately fans out to claude/codex) for resolver uniformity is the intent, leave it but treat it purely as a guard. No behavior depends on it either way.
uncertainty: The signature likely mirrors acp.New for a uniform resolver call shape; if that symmetry is a deliberate convention this is acceptable and not a defect.

message: The exec backend does not document that --sealed / spec.ReadOnly is best-effort: filesystem read-only is NOT enforced (a plain CLI has no sandbox) and network remains available in both grounded and sealed modes. The contract acceptance criterion explicitly requires this be 'documented as trusted/best-effort', but no such comment exists. This is misleading because docs/DESIGN.md frames 'grounded read-only' as a write-rejecting sandbox (true for the ACP backend), so a user passing --sealed for a gemini/agy persona may wrongly assume filesystem writes are prevented.
fix_direction: Add a doc comment (on resolveCwd, Open, or the package doc) stating that for the exec backend spec.ReadOnly/--sealed only selects a fresh empty temp working directory and does NOT enforce filesystem read-only (a plain CLI has no sandbox) — it is trusted/best-effort — and that network remains available in both grounded and sealed modes.

message: In sendOnce the stdin broken-pipe branch (exec.go:190) is evaluated before the success return (exec.go:199) and is not gated on the process exit status. When the subprocess exits zero with valid stdout but closes its stdin read end before Send finishes writing the full reconstructed context, the stdin Write returns EPIPE and sendOnce returns a retryable ErrTransportDrop, discarding the valid stdoutData. The single retry repeats the identical deterministic outcome, so a turn that actually succeeded is surfaced as a transient failure with empty content. This is a distinct failure mode from the accepted proposals p_001/p_003, which address only the non-zero-exit case and the lost stderr; their reorder (broken-pipe-after-non-zero-exit) fix would still leave line 190 discarding a successful zero-exit result.
trigger: A subprocess exits with status 0 and writes valid stdout but closes its stdin read end before Send finishes writing the full reconstructed context (e.g. when the accumulated both-sided context exceeds the OS pipe buffer, ~16 KiB on darwin, which the contract anticipates), so the stdin write returns EPIPE.
fix_direction: Only treat a stdin broken pipe as a retryable drop when there is no successful zero-exit result: check waitErr first and, on a zero exit, return transport.Result{Content: string(stdoutData)} (ignoring a benign EPIPE from the unfinished write); classify broken pipe as ErrTransportDrop only when waitErr indicates a non-success/spawn-level failure. Update TestSend_BrokenPipe_Retried, which currently encodes zero-exit+broken-pipe as a retryable drop.

message: defaultCommandRunner — the only code that actually spawns a subprocess (StdinPipe/StdoutPipe/StderrPipe/Start/Wait wiring) — has no deterministic test coverage. Every unit test injects a fake runner; the sole caller of defaultCommandRunner is New(BackendAgy, ..., nil) inside integration_test.go, which is gated behind //go:build exec_integration AND requires DEBATE_EXEC_INTEGRATION (and AGY_MODEL). The gate runs that test with the env var cleared, so it skips and defaultCommandRunner never executes. A bug in the real pipe wiring would pass the entire default suite. The contract explicitly allows local stub subprocesses in tests ('a fake CLI stub program ... local stub subprocesses in tests are allowed'), so this seam is testable deterministically.
trigger: always — defaultCommandRunner is reachable only through the gated integration test, which the default gate compiles but skips.
fix_direction: Add a deterministic unit test that exercises defaultCommandRunner against a local stub subprocess (e.g. a TestMain re-exec helper, or `sh -c`/`cat`), asserting that reconstructed stdin reaches the process, stdout is captured as Result.Content, stderr is captured, and a non-zero exit maps to a terminal error.

message: The real-EPIPE branch of isBrokenPipe (strings.Contains(err.Error(), "broken pipe")) is never exercised by any runnable test. The fake runner's closeStdinEarly path uses io.Pipe + stdinR.Close(), which produces io.ErrClosedPipe ('io: read/write on closed pipe') and hits only the errors.Is(err, io.ErrClosedPipe) branch. A real subprocess that closes stdin before the write completes produces a *fs.PathError wrapping syscall.EPIPE whose message contains 'broken pipe' — the primary real-world retryable path the contract describes — and that branch has zero coverage. A regression in the string-match branch would not be caught by the gate.
trigger: A real subprocess closes stdin before sendOnce finishes writing, producing a syscall.EPIPE ('...: broken pipe') error rather than io.ErrClosedPipe.
fix_direction: Add a test that drives isBrokenPipe (directly with a syscall.EPIPE-backed *fs.PathError, or end-to-end via a stub subprocess that exits/closes stdin early) and assert the resulting Send error classifies as retryable.

message: outcomeString is a no-op wrapper: every switch branch (the explicit settled/stalemate/max case and the default) returns the input `reason` unchanged, so the function is an identity function. Its doc comment claims it 'normalises the loop reason to a user-facing string,' but no normalization happens. It is a wrapper that adds nothing / an unused extension point. This is pre-existing code (this change only added the exec import and the `case exec.BackendAgy` resolver branch to cmd/debate/main.go), so it is advisory and non-blocking.
fix_direction: Either remove outcomeString and pass result.Outcome.Reason directly at the call site, or implement the normalization the comment promises if a divergence between internal and user-facing reasons is actually intended.

message: docs/DESIGN.md presents 'grounded read-only' as a guaranteed write-rejecting sandbox for all grounded agents, but the exec/agy backend wired into production by this change enforces no read-only at all (resolveCwd only selects the working directory; a plain CLI has no sandbox). The user-facing design doc is not updated to note the exec exception, so a user running a gemini/agy persona in grounded (non-sealed) mode would wrongly assume the project is protected from writes. This is the user-facing-doc counterpart of the code-comment gap tracked by f_009/p_009.
trigger: A user runs a gemini/agy-backed persona in grounded (non-sealed) mode expecting the documented read-only sandbox; agy has no sandbox and can write/mutate files in the project cwd.
fix_direction: Update docs/DESIGN.md's grounded-read-only section and the Locked-decisions bullet to note the exec/agy backend does not enforce read-only — for exec, filesystem read-only is trusted/best-effort with no sandbox; only acp enforces write-rejection. Complements the code-comment fix in f_009/p_009.

message: docs/DESIGN.md documents --sealed as 'дебат только по брифу: без чтения проекта/сети' (debate from the brief only, without reading the project OR the network), but the exec/agy backend's sealed mode only swaps the working directory to a fresh empty temp dir and does not disable network. This change's own contract states 'network is available in both modes', directly contradicting the doc. Pre-existing: the acp backend shipped the same sealed behavior in Slice 5, so the doc was already inaccurate; this change reinforces it for a second backend.
trigger: A user passes --sealed for a gemini/agy (or acp) persona expecting network to be disabled; network remains available in sealed mode.
fix_direction: Update docs/DESIGN.md to state that --sealed isolates the working directory (no project files) but does NOT disable network for the acp/exec backends; correct or qualify the 'без ... сети' wording.
